item.title: Anchovy fillets
item.description: Salt-cured Engraulis encrasicolus fillets in olive oil. The Mediterranean kitchen's primary glutamate-and-IMP umami source, used as background savory builder rather than fish flavor.

item.title: Basil
item.description: Ocimum basilicum. The defining summer herb of Mediterranean and Southeast Asian cuisines. Volatile-oil-rich (linalool, eugenol, methyl chavicol), heat-fragile, oxidation-prone, a finishing herb in nearly every application.

item.title: Blood orange
item.description: Citrus × sinensis cultivars (Tarocco, Moro, Sanguinello) whose flesh accumulates anthocyanin pigments under cool-night growing conditions. Crimson-streaked, more acidic than common navel, with raspberry-and-bitter-orange aromatics.

item.title: Capers
item.description: Unopened flower buds of Capparis spinosa, salt-packed or brined. The Mediterranean kitchen's primary mustard-pungent finishing pickle, dense in glucosinolates that release on chewing.

item.title: Dijon mustard
item.description: Smooth pale-yellow mustard from Dijon, France: brown mustard seeds (Brassica juncea) ground in verjuice or white wine. Pungent, mucilaginous, the canonical French emulsifier-and-acid in vinaigrettes.

item.title: Feta
item.description: Greek brined sheep's-milk cheese (PDO since 2002), made from at least 70 percent sheep's milk with up to 30 percent goat. Crumbly, lactic-tangy, salt-cured in brine for 2 to 3 months. The defining cheese of Greek and broader Eastern Mediterranean cooking.

item.title: Greek yogurt
item.description: Whole-milk yogurt strained through cloth to remove a substantial fraction of the whey. Concentrated protein (10 percent), thicker texture, tangier flavor than American-style yogurt. Structural ingredient in dips, sauces, and Eastern Mediterranean cooking.

item.title: Haricots verts
item.description: Phaseolus vulgaris bred for slim pods, picked young at 3-4 mm cross-section. Thinner walls, faster cook, more sugar than commodity green beans.

item.title: Honey
item.description: Concentrated nectar processed by honeybees (Apis mellifera). Roughly 80 percent sugar (mostly glucose and fructose, in varying ratios that determine crystallization behavior), 17 percent water, and a small fraction of acids, enzymes, and flavor compounds inherited from the foraged flowers.

item.title: Lemon
item.description: Citrus limon. The kitchen's primary bright finishing acid (5 to 6 percent citric acid in the juice) and aromatic carrier (limonene-rich oil in the zest).

item.title: Mint
item.description: Aromatic herb in the Mentha genus; spearmint (Mentha spicata) is the cooking-canonical species, peppermint (M. × piperita) the candy-and-cocktail one. Defined aromatic comes from monoterpene compounds — carvone in spearmint, menthol in peppermint.

item.title: Nicoise olives
item.description: Cailletier-cultivar olives from the Var and Alpes-Maritimes regions of southeastern France, brine-cured with herbs. Small, dark-purple to black, with a high pit-to-flesh ratio and a wine-like cured note.

item.title: Pistachio
item.description: Seed of Pistacia vera, a desert-tree drupe split between Iranian, Sicilian (Bronte DOP), Turkish (Antep), and California production. Bright green flesh from chlorophyll, distinctive resinous pine-and-floral aromatics from terpene-derived compounds.

item.title: Red beet
item.description: Beta vulgaris taproot, deep red-purple from betacyanin pigments. Sweetest of the common roots (8 to 12 percent sugar by weight) with the distinct earthy character of geosmin.

item.title: Red wine vinegar
item.description: Acetic-acid solution (5 to 7 percent) produced by acetobacter fermentation of red wine. The default Mediterranean vinaigrette acid, with structural notes from the underlying wine.

item.title: Shallot
item.description: Allium ascalonicum (or A. cepa var. aggregatum). Smaller, milder, more aromatic-complex than yellow onion, with a higher sugar fraction and a softer sulfur profile.

item.title: Tomato (fresh)
item.description: Solanum lycopersicum fruit, eaten ripe. Free-glutamate concentration runs 100 to 250 mg per 100 g: the second-highest natural source after parmigiano. The umami driver of Mediterranean cooking when ripe; a watery footnote out of season.

item.title: Tuna loin
item.description: Skinless, boneless block from the dorsal muscle of yellowfin, bigeye, albacore, or bluefin tuna. Lean, deep-red, mostly slow-twitch muscle, with myoglobin levels that run higher than any other commodity fish.

item.title: Urfa biber
item.description: Turkish dried red chili from the Urfa (Şanlıurfa) region, sun-cured by day and cloth-wrapped by night. Distinctive dark-burgundy color, raisin-cocoa-tobacco aromatics, and a slow, low-heat finish.

item.title: Waxy potato
item.description: Low-starch, high-moisture potato cultivars (fingerling, new potato, La Ratte, red bliss, Yukon Gold). Cell walls hold their structure through boiling: the right potato for salads, gratins, and any dish that needs the cube to stay a cube.

item.title: Salade niçoise
item.description: Oil-poached tuna, blanched haricots verts, jammy egg, salt-cured tomato, and nicoise olives over an anchovy-dijon vinaigrette. Composed, not tossed; every component salted on its own terms.

item.title: Roasted beet and citrus salad with whipped feta
item.description: Salt-roasted red beets, segmented blood orange, urfa-warmed pistachio, and a feta-yogurt cream whipped to spreading texture. Composed warm-on-cold; the dressing is the cheese itself.

item.title: Jammy egg
item.description: Boil a fridge-cold large egg for 6 minutes 30 seconds, ice-shock immediately. The white sets fully (62 to 70 °C internal); the yolk holds at the chalky-cream threshold (about 68 °C): set enough to hold a dome at the cut, soft enough to weep slightly when broken.

item.title: Oil poaching
item.description: Cook a protein submerged in oil held at 55 to 75 °C: gentle, even, with no surface evaporation. The oil becomes both heat medium and infusion vehicle; the protein arrives uniformly tender with no Maillard crust.

item.title: Salt-bed roasting
item.description: Roast a dense vegetable or whole fish on a 1 to 2 cm bed of coarse salt at 180 to 220 °C. The salt conducts heat evenly to the underside, draws surface moisture out, and prevents scorching from dripping juices. Concentrates flavor without seasoning the interior.

item.title: Vinaigrette emulsion
item.description: Stream oil into a vinegar-and-mustard base while whisking: produces a temporary oil-in-water emulsion that holds long enough to dress a salad. The mustard provides the surface-active agents that bridge oil and water; without an emulsifier, the dressing breaks within seconds.

item.title: Atta flour (whole wheat chapati flour, آٹا)
item.description: Indian-style whole wheat flour milled from durum-style hard wheat, with all the bran and germ retained, ground extra-fine for soft-pliable dough. The structural flour for roti, chapati, paratha, and naan. Lower gluten than American whole wheat flour, higher protein than all-purpose, fundamentally different milling.

item.title: Bananas
item.description: Fruit of *Musa acuminata* / *M. balbisiana* hybrids, dominated by Cavendish in global trade. The starch-to-sugar conversion curve over a few days at room temperature is the single most important variable for cooking.

item.title: Bay leaf (tej patta, तेज पत्ता)
item.description: Dried leaves of Laurus nobilis (European bay) or Cinnamomum tamala (Indian bay, also called tej patta or malabar leaf). Used whole as an aromatic in long-simmered dishes. Indian bay is structurally different from European bay: closer to cinnamon-clove than to camphor-pine.

item.title: Bell pepper
item.description: Capsicum annuum without the capsaicin: sweet pepper varieties bred to lack the heat-producing compound that makes their chile cousins spicy. Color (green to red) marks ripeness and shifts both sugar and aroma profile.
